tests.description: Clearing the account-session store forgets the stored bearer token.
An empty stored value reads back as nil (no session), never an empty bearer.
A stored session token round-trips through the account-session store unchanged.
The transport's token provider returns the stored token, or nil when none so requests go out unauthenticated.
Turning health sync off with keep-data does not delete the remote health row.
Turning health sync off with delete removes the remote health row and clears the opt-in.
A guest never pushes health data even when the health opt-in is on.
A guest never pushes preferences even when settings sync is enabled.
Health never uploads while the health opt-in is off, even for a signed-in user.
A SyncableHealthSnapshot encodes and decodes back to an equal value.
Health sync cannot be enabled without first acknowledging the consent step.
Health uploads only when signed in and the health opt-in is on.
With equal timestamps local wins and nothing is pushed when the values match.
Last-write-wins: a local-newer merge keeps and pushes the local value.
Last-write-wins: with no remote row the local value wins and is pushed.
Last-write-wins: a remote-newer merge adopts the remote value.
A SyncablePreferences encodes and decodes back to an equal value.
Account deletion purges the remote health row and forgets both sync opt-ins.
Reconcile adopts remote preferences when the remote row is newer than local.
Settings push is blocked when the settings-sync toggle is off.
Settings push happens when signed in and settings sync is enabled.
Active-minutes bins by duration, ranking a slow long walk above a fast short run.
Any activity is at least level 1 so a short recovery walk never reads as a rest day.
Day one yields a rectangle with nothing active, driving the friendly empty state.
A day with no workouts is reported as a rest day, not an error.
An unparseable date yields nil so the view can clear the selection.
A tapped day reports only that day's workouts plus its mileage and minute totals.
Distance bins by miles relative to the busiest visible day; rest days stay level 0.
Every range renders full 7-day Monday-start weeks so the grid is always rectangular.
Month labels appear only where the month changes, naming each month once in order.
A single-week span still names its month.
Range changes the visible span only, keeping day-granular cells.
Multiple workouts on one day sum into a single cell.
Steps bin against the daily goal, not the window max, and clamp at the top level.
With no seeded step series the steps metric is empty; workouts never leak into it.
Unparseable workout dates are dropped, matching ActivityHistory.groupByWeek.
An unparseable anchor date yields no grid rather than silently anchoring on now.
Only the in-progress week is capped at elapsed days; a finished week keeps its honest 7 minus activeDays so history is not retroactively rewritten.
Workouts in different weeks produce separate groups, newest week first.
An empty workout list yields no week groups.
Workouts with an unparseable date are dropped rather than crashing.
The series is capped to maxWeeks (most recent weeks kept).
daysRun counts DISTINCT run days, not run workouts, and ignores non-runs.
No workouts yields an empty series (not shared with the coach).
The series has one point per week, newest week first, with summed mileage.
The model decodes a seeded rbWorkoutsJSON list into the today state.
With no rbWorkoutsJSON seeded, the workouts list is empty.
Rest days are seven minus the number of distinct active days.
A full seven distinct active days leaves zero rest days, never negative.
Resting today after running earlier counts only the completed rest days, leaving today uncounted while it is still open.
A Saturday/Sunday/Monday block is barely visible in the calendar week on Tuesday, but the rolling 7-day window still sees all three run days. This is the signal that stops the false quiet week.
The rolling window is inclusive of the reference day and stops 7 days back, so a same-day run counts while an 8-day-old run is excluded.
Run count includes only run-type workouts, not walks or rides.
Workouts in the same ISO week collapse into one group.
A seeded weekly load derives its elapsed and trailing-window fields from the seeded workouts, so a scenario can show the in-progress and rolling-window states the real HealthKit path produces.
A seeded load with no workouts keeps the default elapsed and rolling-window values, so existing load-only Weekly Review scenarios are unaffected.
Today's mileage and runs count immediately, but today is not a rest day until it is over; a run logged today must not cancel out genuinely rested earlier days.
Total mileage sums every workout in the week.
weeklyLoad with no workouts is an all-rest, steady week with nothing logged.
Activity this week with NO prior history is also "insufficient" — we don't know the user's baseline yet, so we abstain instead of calling it "building".
Too little history to form a baseline (one prior week) is honest "insufficient" rather than a guessed verdict.
The SAME down-week data early in the week (asOf Wed) is NOT called "recovering" — the partial week just hasn't accumulated yet (mid-week guard).

A normal week following a deliberate CUTBACK week is NOT a false spike. The old 2-week ratio (18/12 = 1.5) tripped "spiking"; the multi-week baseline (18 vs ~17.3 avg) correctly reads it as steady. This is the reported bug.
A deliberate down week vs. a solid baseline reads "recovering" — but only once the week is mostly elapsed (asOf Sunday).
weeklyLoad rolls up mileage, longest run, run-days and rest-days from only the week containing asOf.
Three prior weeks at a flat baseline, then a sudden 2x jump this week, is a GENUINE spike against the multi-week baseline.
A steady, progressive climb reads as "building", NOT "spiking" — the core trustworthy-coaching fix (a ~10-25% rise above the baseline is the plan working).
Within a week, workouts are ordered newest-first by date.
Apple .authorized credential state maps to .authorized.
Apple .notFound credential state maps to .notFound.
Apple .revoked credential state maps to .revoked.
Apple .transferred credential state maps to .unknown so the session is retained.
Caution-leaning moods carry distinct accent colors from the upbeat default.
Every mood exposes a non-empty caption used by the mood chip.
A known raw string maps to the matching mood case, case-insensitively.
An unknown or empty raw string falls back to the friendly ready default.
The closing check-in question rotates across turns and does not repeat the question the coach just asked.
Anything unrecognized falls back to the general intent.
Pain/injury wording routes to the injury intent regardless of other words.
Mileage-ramp wording routes to the mileage intent.
Reflection wording routes to the reflection intent.
Run-vs-rest wording routes to the run-or-rest intent.
Mild post-run soreness routes to the calm soreness intent while sharp or worsening pain still routes to the injury lockdown.
Step-goal wording routes to the hit-10K intent.
Below goal with nothing else notable, dailyNudge returns a gentle walk nudge naming the remaining steps.
dailyNudge in a declared, progressing build affirms the trajectory (a run-day "on track" nudge), not a rest nudge, and is not flagged.
When the step goal is met, dailyNudge returns a celebratory, non-safety-flagged nudge (buddyMood celebrating).
dailyNudge under thin history is an honest "still learning" nudge, never flagged.
A recent hard run with no load spike yields a recovery/rest nudge that is not safety-flagged.
A spiking weekly load takes priority and yields a safety-flagged caution nudge (buddyMood recovery).
A walking-focused profile (no other training) personalizes the below-goal walk nudge with walking-is-your-training copy, staying safety-neutral.
Without a walking-focused profile (none, or one that includes running) the walk nudge keeps its original generic copy; personalization is additive.
A routine easy 5-mile run is no longer treated as hard, so the reply stays go-ahead rather than forcing recovery.
The general "what should I do" answer under thin history keeps it simple and safe.
A genuinely hard long run with no spike still steers the reply to recovery, so the guardrail is dialed back not removed.
An injury question is always safety-flagged, concerned, and never diagnoses.
A declared build with a modest rise affirms the build instead of advising rest — the reported bug fixed — and is not safety-flagged.
A genuine spike overrides the declared build: caution and the safety flag win.
With thin history the mileage answer abstains honestly rather than guessing, and is not safety-flagged.
A spiking weekly load makes the mileage answer cautionary and safety-flagged.
With a steady load the mileage answer is reassuring, not flagged.
Genuine, worsening pain still triggers the clinician-caution lockdown with the safety flag.
Reflection with a logged workout cites the run and is upbeat.
After a hard run or spiking load, run-or-rest leans to recovery.
Run-or-rest under thin history defers to how the user feels, not a hard verdict.
When fresh, run-or-rest allows an easy run.
Mild post-run soreness gets calm reassurance with no safety flag and a non-concerned mood.
Below goal, the steps answer names the remaining steps and stays ready.
At or past goal, the steps answer celebrates instead of nudging.
clear removes the stored coach key.
With no key saved, the coach key store reports not-connected.
Saving a key trims surrounding whitespace and reports connected.
A whitespace-only entry trims to empty, so it is treated as no key.
CoachProfileStore.clear removes the stored profile so a subsequent load is empty again.
Back-compat: a profile JSON written before trainingPhase existed (no such key) decodes with trainingPhase == nil rather than failing.
A never-stored CoachProfile loads as the empty default (walkVolume/walkTime nil, otherTraining empty).
Encoded CoachProfile JSON uses enum raw values and omits skipped (nil) fields, matching the coach backend shape.
The encoded payload carries the phase as its raw value for the backend coach.
A fully-filled CoachProfile survives save then load unchanged and is non-empty.
isEmpty is true only for an all-nil/empty profile; any single set field makes it non-empty.
A partially-filled CoachProfile (a skipped question stored as nil) round-trips and is still considered non-empty.
A declared training phase alone makes the profile non-empty, so a user who only sets their phase still reaches the coach context.
The training phase survives save -> load unchanged alongside the other fields.
Accessibility aliases map to the matching accessibility sizes.
Every accessibility size maps to one flagged as an accessibility size.
The mapping is case-insensitive.
An empty seed honors the system size, returning nil.
Standard size aliases map to the matching standard sizes.
An unrecognized value returns nil rather than a wrong size.
formatted groups thousands with separators for readable step counts.
formatted leaves small numbers and zero untouched.
movementLabel rolls into hours, dropping the minutes part when it is zero.
movementLabel uses bare minutes under an hour.
movementLabel reads now at or below zero minutes since moving.
prettyDate returns the input unchanged when it is not a valid ISO date.
prettyDate turns a valid ISO date into a short weekday and day label.
stepGoalAccessibilityValue celebrates a crushed goal when steps exceed the goal.
stepGoalAccessibilityValue spells out remaining steps when the goal is not yet met.
stepGoalAccessibilityValue announces the goal reached at exactly the goal count.
stepGoalCaption gives extra cheer once the goal is passed; exceeded outranks reached.
stepGoalCaption frames the goal target when the goal has not been reached yet.
stepGoalCaption celebrates exactly hitting the goal with a goal-hit line.
stepRingFill clamps a met goal to exactly a full ring.
stepRingFill caps a crushed goal above one at a full ring with no overdraw.
stepRingFill floors at a tiny positive value so the rounded cap shows at zero percent.
stepRingFill passes typical mid-progress fractions through unchanged.
A finished week renders a plain rollup with no 'so far' qualifier.
An in-progress week is qualified with 'so far' so its smaller numbers read as a week still being lived rather than one that went badly.
Run count is singular at exactly 1 and plural otherwise, including 0.
A finished week's spoken VoiceOver form stays plain, with plural rest days.
The spoken form pluralizes rest days and states how far into the week we are, so a screen-reader user gets the same context the visual 'so far' conveys.
Whole mileage drops the decimal, matching the shared miles() helper.
connect() on a seeded source ends up authorized and loads the seeded steps.
A model built from a connected state reports authorized; an empty one does not.
A launch with no rb* preferences is production, not a seeded scenario.
A launch with any rb* preference is treated as a seeded CodeYam scenario.
The seeded health source reports authorized only once rbConnected is set.
The seeded health source loads the seeded snapshot and marks it connected.
Requesting authorization on the seeded health source grants and marks connected.
A known raw string maps to the matching tab, case-insensitively
An unknown or empty raw string falls back to today, the default landing tab
clampGoal pins out-of-range goals to the min and max bounds and rounds in-range values to the nearest 500 increment.
Connecting Apple Health flips the model from the day-one hero into the dashboard.
The empty production default is the not-yet-connected day-one state that shows the Connect hero.
UserPreferences falls back to the default step goal when none is set, and persists a set goal.
goalExceeded is true only past the goal, never at or below it, and never for a zero goal.
Goal progress is the steps over goal ratio clamped to 1.0, and steps-remaining never goes negative.
Ingesting an empty workout list is a no-op and fabricates no weekly load.
Importing Strava runs also populates the multi-week loadHistory series the coaches reason from, not just the single weekly-load flag.
Importing Strava runs rolls them into the weekly load so a Strava-only user gets a real recap instead of the empty first-week prompt.
isPreset returns true for a quick-preset goal value and false for a custom non-preset value.
An explicit rbLoadHistoryJSON seeds the coach-facing weekly series verbatim, newest week first.
The onboarding profile seeds into TodayState via rbCoachProfileJSON; an all-empty profile blob leaves TodayState.profile nil.
The declared training phase seeds through rbCoachProfileJSON alongside the other fields, so it reaches the coach context from launch.
With no explicit series but a seeded workout list, loadHistory is DERIVED from the workouts, so a rich multi-week scenario gets a real trajectory.
With no keys seeded the reader yields the empty disconnected state with a default 10k goal.
Flat rb preference keys are read back into a fully-populated TodayState including the coach group.
With neither key seeded, loadHistory is empty (not shared with the coach).
Setting a non-preset custom goal updates the live TodayState and persists it through UserPreferences.
Setting the daily step goal applies it to the dashboard immediately and persists it.
Setting the phase persists it through CoachProfileStore and applies it to the dashboard immediately; clearing with nil removes it.
Setting the phase preserves the profile's other fields (walk habits, other training) rather than clobbering them.
A TodayState JSON written before loadHistory existed still decodes, with the series defaulting to empty (tolerant decode).
Legacy TodayState scenario JSON without a profile field still decodes, defaulting the optional profile to nil (back-compat).
A non-positive hours setting is floored to 1h so the nudge never fires in the past.
Last movement just now with a 3h setting pushes the nudge out the full 3h.
Last movement 2h ago with a 3h setting fires the nudge about 1h from now.
No known movement yields no fire date, so nothing is scheduled.
A last movement long enough ago that the nudge is overdue clamps to fire promptly.
With the inactivity reminder off, re-arm clears any pending nudge and never schedules.
Re-arm computes an absolute fire date from the user's real last movement rather than cancelling.
Absent the explicit key, the seeded source falls back to rbMinutesSinceMovement.
rbLastMovementMinutesAgo places the seeded last movement that many minutes back.
With neither movement key seeded, the source reports no known movement.
A newly added onboarding or welcome scenario must be listed in the expected-page table so it cannot be introduced with an unchecked page index.
Every seeded page must be addressable so a stale seed is never silently clamped by startPage onto a neighbouring screen.
Every onboarding and welcome scenario's seeded rbOnboardingPage must match the step it is named for, so a capture cannot silently render a different screen than its name claims.
Pins the intro and personalization step counts so adding or removing an onboarding step fails loudly and forces the scenario page indices to be updated in the same change.
First launch (nothing seen, not seeded) shows the onboarding tour.
A scenario-seeded run skips the onboarding tour by default.
After markSeen(), the onboarding tour never auto-shows again.
startPage reads and clamps rbOnboardingPage into the valid range.
startScreen == 'onboarding' forces the tour even when already seen (preview/replay).
The flow models the three intro pages plus four personalization steps, with pageCount kept as a back-compat alias for the intro count.
deregisterAll issues a DELETE to /account/push (the sign-out / health-off opt-out).
The movement heartbeat fields round-trip through Codable and default to nil.
Without a bearer session, register is a no-op that fires no request (opt-in gate).
A signed-in register POSTs to /account/push with the bearer and reports success on 2xx.
A non-2xx response is a soft failure returning false, leaving the app functional.
The local nudge is suppressed only when signed in, health sync on, and push registered all hold.
Classifies whether a chat message expresses race intent.
daysUntil computes the day count from now to a race date.
Distance presets resolve and clamp into the supported range.
A general coaching reply is race-aware even for a fresh user.
hasUpcoming returns true only when at least one race is on or after today, and false for empty or past-only race sets — the Today add-a-race banner gate.
A km-entered race stores full-precision miles and displays back as the exact km value plus its miles equivalent.
Converts a typed distance value in miles or kilometers to clamped miles, keeping km at full precision so it round-trips.
Race-aware coaching replies reflect the correct training phase.
Read state decodes seeded races from the scenario seed.
The race store round-trips persisted races and applies mutations.
Computes the upcoming races and the single next race.
The weekly review folds in taper guidance when a race is near.
A spiking-load safety signal keeps precedence over race messaging in the weekly review.
connect() re-merges the persisted coach profile onto the freshly loaded HealthKit snapshot.
connect() re-merges on-device races onto the HealthKit snapshot so added races survive a load.
A sparse import draft seeds the editor with sensible defaults for missing fields, inventing nothing.
A full import draft preserves name, date, km unit, location and notes into the editor seed.
With no on-device profile stored, the re-merge leaves today.profile nil after a load.
A race added this session survives a refresh() that reloads the raceless HealthKit snapshot.
A search result maps to a draft and exposes a stable list id from name, date and location.
A fresh install has every reminder off, with sensible default times.
An explicitly-set midnight hour 0 is preserved, not mistaken for unset.
save() then load() round-trips every reminder field exactly.
A 400 maps to a server error, not invalidKey, so it never blames the user key.
A 401 is the only status that maps to invalidKey.
A 429 maps to the rateLimited error.
A 200 with malformed JSON is a server error so the caller falls back to the mock.
A 200 with a valid body decodes into a CoachReply.
A stored credential that is still authorized keeps the user signed in across relaunch.
Continuing as guest is remembered so the app does not re-prompt on the next launch.
Deleting the account forgets the identity and the guest choice, returning to the welcome screen.
A previously-saved Apple identifier resumes the signed-in state on launch.
Production with no saved identity and no guest choice shows the sign-in screen.
A not-found Apple identifier is treated like revocation and drops the session to guest.
Revalidation is a no-op for a guest and never triggers a credential check.
A genuinely revoked credential drops the session to guest mode rather than the welcome screen.
A normal seeded scenario skips sign-in so previews go straight to content.
Signing in persists the Apple identifier and moves to the signed-in state.
A scenario can opt in to preview the sign-in welcome screen.
Signing out forgets the Apple identity but keeps the app usable as a guest.
An offline or unknown credential-check result keeps the user signed in; transient failures never log out.
Within the revalidation window, no second credential check runs, so the session stays signed in without churn.
Picking a theme persists it to UserDefaults until the user changes it again.
With nothing saved and no scenario seed, the store opens on the Default theme.
Every ThemeID resolves to a matching token set with a name, blurb, and correct isDark.
A returning user loads the theme they last chose as their personal default.
The rbTheme scenario seed wins over the saved value so any theme is capturable.
An unrecognized saved theme value degrades to Default rather than crashing.
Every activity review fills all five sections plus a focus area.
A building week is celebratory and not safety-flagged.
A cross-training runner keeps the run-oriented copy and gets the cross-training named in the what-changed section.
The same context always yields an identical review.
A profile-bearing context still yields a fully deterministic review.
An empty all-skipped profile is a no-op, leaving the recap identical to the no-profile baseline.

With too little history to judge, the review recaps the week but WITHHOLDS a confident training-risk verdict and is not safety-flagged ("no coaching over bad coaching"). It is distinct from the spiking/sparse/solid classifications.
The gathering review still recaps the actual mileage/run count that was logged.
With no weekly load at all, the review is the encouraging first-week prompt.
A single-run, mostly-rest week reads gently and is never safety-flagged.
A spiking load is safety-flagged, concerned, and escalates real warning signs.
Personalization never softens the spiking-week safety copy; the review is identical with or without a profile.
Spiking wins even when several runs went in; the load trend dominates.
A steady week is positive and unflagged, with steady-specific framing.
A walking-focused profile reframes the solid-week recap toward walks-as-training while keeping mood and safety unchanged.
A walking-focused profile reframes the sparse-week recap to speak of movement sessions rather than runs.
A load object with zero mileage and zero runs still counts as no activity.
A zero-run week that still logged mileage is treated as sparse, not empty.
